story:
I hear him before I see him: the low hum of a motor, the faint clink of bottles threading through a night that still holds its breath. I slip from my warm bed, careful not to wake my sister, and creep to my second-story window—my magic window—where I claim this small hour as mine alone. Down the quiet avenue rolls the Winder Dairy milk truck, its cab lit by a single golden bulb that makes an ordinary job look almost holy. From my perch, I watch him gather his treasures—gallons of creamy milk, a dozen eggs, cool butter that will whirl into pancake batter, sometimes bread or cottage cheese—and head around the side of the house toward the metal box by our back door. With the food comes something I need even more: steadiness. The promise that, no matter what the daylight tries to take from us, something faithful arrives in the dark.

In a house of five children, especially as the middle one, everything is shared—space, food, attention. This quiet ritual is the one thing that belongs only to me. In daylight, it would look small and silly, but in the hush before morning, it fills me with warmth and ease, as if the whole world were being set gently in place.

Lately, though, I notice things I used to miss. The grocer, Mr. Anast, slipping a box of Sweets Chocolate Orange Sticks into our bag, his wife smiling kindly, “Don’t worry, you can pay next week.” Those small mercies carry a shadow, a hint that my parents are juggling more than I understand. And then there is Tanya.

We met when we were five and have been side by side ever since. She lives just down the street. We spend summers in the sugar beet field with kites tugging our wrists, send tin-can stilts clacking around the block, launch newspaper boats into the irrigation canal, sell “fish pond” surprises from upstairs windows. Her dad taught us to shoot arrows at a hay bale target. We planned eighth grade together—new school clothes, practice for ninth-grade cheerleader tryouts, the future bright and braided tight between us. But as school began, Tanya faded. She grew yellow, tired, absent. Even her fourteenth birthday couldn’t lift her. The word came down like a stone: leukemia. I don’t really understand what it means, but I know it’s serious. I pray every night, asking if God can hear me, if He will make her well.

And still, right on time, the milkman arrives. Before he turns toward our back door, he lifts his face to my dark second-story window and says softly, “Yes, He can hear you, and He knows you’re asking for Tanya,” then moves from house to house, sure and quiet, a small constellation of clinks and soft steps tracing hope along our street. For a moment I ask if he can do miracles. Then I realize he already has. His constancy is its own miracle, delivering comfort to a girl who needs something to believe in.

Before I slip back under my covers, I hold the scene inside me: the glow, the gifts, the quiet certainty that we are being cared for. I thank God for the angels among us, for food on the table, for the courage to wait. I pray for Tanya again. Morning will come. I’ll walk down to see her, sip my mother’s potato soup brightened by fresh butter, play with my sisters, maybe read them a story. Perhaps this secret doesn’t need to be hidden anymore. Perhaps it is something I can share: the simple wonder that arrives in the dark and says, in a few quiet words, everything will be okay.
